title: White tin loaf, overnight cold-proof
subtitle: A 65% dough, mixed the night before
excerpt: A 2 lb tin loaf with a soft crumb and a thin crackling crust. Mix the dough after dinner, fridge it overnight, shape and bake in the morning. The slow cold proof builds the flavour the recipe takes its time on.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 (the tin-baked household loaf as the default British white). Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg (the slow-rise method; Acton was the first widely-read British cookery writer to weight her flour). Florence White, Good Things in England (1932) carries the household-tin tradition through to the 20th century. The 65% hydration and the overnight cold proof are the modern adaptation, drawing on the same straight-dough principle.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice cool, freeze flat in a bag for up to 3 months. Toast straight from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix the dough after dinner; bake from the cold-proofed dough the next morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; drop to 210°C after 10 minutes
baking.steamMethod: water tray on the oven floor for the first 10 minutes

recipeTools.notes: Worth checking your oven once — most home ovens run 20°C either side of the dial.

glossaryTerms.definition: A way of writing a bread recipe where every ingredient is expressed as a percentage of the flour weight. Flour is 100%; water at 65% means 325 g for a 500 g flour anchor.
Stretch a piece of dough between your fingers. If it thins to a translucent membrane without tearing, the gluten is developed enough to trap gas.

content.text: One 2 lb tin loaf, soft inside, with a thin crackling crust. The dough is straight, with no preferment and no levain, and runs at 65% hydration. That's the household-tin sweet spot: enough water for a soft crumb, not so much the dough sticks to the bench. The work is in two short sessions split by a long fridge rest. Mix and knead after dinner; bake in the morning. The cold retard does the flavour work the recipe takes its time on.
Plan for 25 minutes of active work the night before, a 90-minute bulk ferment at room temperature, an overnight cold proof, then 35 minutes in the oven the next day. The bake is the easy part.

attrs.title: Why cold-proof a tin loaf
attrs.body: Yeast slows in the fridge but doesn't stop. Over eight to ten hours of cold the flavour deepens, the crumb tightens slightly, and the gluten relaxes. A loaf retarded overnight has more in common with a sourdough than with a same-day yeasted bread — without the starter maintenance.

content.text: What you need

Scales matter here. The recipe is written in baker's percentages: water at 65% of the flour weight, salt at 2%, yeast at 1.4%. If you swap to a different flour weight (250 g for a 1 lb tin; 1000 g for two loaves), keep the percentages and the dough behaves the same way.
Method
Mix and rest, 10 minutes

Weigh {{strong-bread-flour}} of strong bread flour into a large mixing bowl. Whisk in {{yeast-dried}} of dried yeast on one side and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt on the other; the direct contact between yeast and salt before water joins them slows the rise. Pour in {{water}} of tepid water and stir with the handle of a wooden spoon until no dry flour remains and the dough comes together as a shaggy mass. Cover the bowl with a plate and leave for 10 minutes. The flour finishes hydrating without any kneading; the dough you come back to will already feel smoother.
Knead, 8 minutes

Tip the dough onto an unfloured worktop. Lift the far edge, pull it towards you over the body of the dough, and press it down with the heel of your hand; rotate the dough a quarter turn and repeat. The first minute is sticky and feels wrong. Keep going — after three minutes the dough comes off the worktop in one piece, and after eight it pulls into a smooth ball that springs back when you press a fingertip into it. Tear off a hazelnut-sized piece and stretch it between your thumbs; if it thins to a translucent membrane without tearing, the gluten is developed. If it tears, knead for another two minutes and test again.
Bulk ferment, 90 minutes

Shape the dough into a ball, return it to the bowl, cover with a damp tea towel, and leave at room temperature. After 90 minutes it should be roughly doubled — push a floured finger 1 cm into the dough; the dent should fill back slowly but not vanish. Warm kitchens go faster; cool ones go slower. Use the dough, not the clock.
Shape and tin

Grease a 2 lb loaf tin lightly. Turn the dough onto the worktop, press it gently into a rectangle the long-side length of your tin, and roll it up tightly from the short end, sealing the seam with the heel of your hand at each turn. Drop the loaf seam-side-down into the tin and press the top flat with your knuckles.
Cold retard, overnight

Slide the tin into a clean plastic bag, leaving room above the dough for it to rise, and put it in the fridge. Eight to ten hours is the sweet spot; up to twelve is fine. Beyond twelve the dough loses tension and the loaf flattens.
Final proof, 45–60 minutes

In the morning, take the tin out of the fridge and let it come up to room temperature on the worktop. The dough should crest 2 cm above the rim of the tin and feel light when you nudge the tin sideways. While it warms, heat the oven to 230°C fan and set a deep metal tray on the oven floor to heat with it.
Bake, 35 minutes

Slide the tin onto the middle shelf. Pour 250 ml of just-boiled water into the hot tray on the oven floor (keep your face clear of the rush of steam) and shut the door fast. Bake for 10 minutes, drop the temperature to 210°C fan, and bake for another 25 minutes. The loaf is done when the top is deep gold, the sides shrink slightly from the tin, and a probe pushed through the side reads 96°C. If you don't have a probe: turn the loaf out of the tin and tap the base; a baked loaf sounds hollow.
Cool, 30 minutes

Turn the loaf out onto a wire rack and leave it for at least 30 minutes. Cutting a hot loaf collapses the crumb; cutting a warm loaf is fine. The crust softens as the loaf cools — for a crisper finish, leave it uncovered on the rack for another hour.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: The four ways a tin loaf goes wrong, and what to fix next time.

items.symptom: Flat loaf with a dense crumb
items.cause: Over-proofed in the fridge — past twelve hours the dough loses tension.
items.fix: Pull the tin out at the eight-hour mark next time and let it final-proof for the full hour before baking.

items.symptom: Tall loaf that splits along the side
items.cause: Under-proofed — the dough hadn't finished rising when it hit the heat.
items.fix: Wait until the dough crests 2 cm above the tin rim AND feels light when you nudge it sideways. The second cue is the one to trust.

items.cause: Oven running cold OR not enough steam in the first 10 minutes.
items.fix: Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more — an oven thermometer takes the guessing out. If the temperature checks out, refill the steam tray with more boiling water on the way in.

items.cause: Tin under-greased, or the loaf left too long after the bake.
items.fix: Grease the tin with a thin film of softened butter and dust with flour next time. Turn the baked loaf out within a minute of leaving the oven.
Wait 30 minutes after the bake before slicing. If the gum persists, the loaf needed another five minutes; an internal probe should read 96°C.

content.text: Variations

A wholemeal version takes 350 g strong bread flour plus 150 g wholemeal bread flour and an extra 15 ml of water — wholemeal drinks more. A seeded version takes a tablespoon each of sunflower seeds, linseeds and sesame seeds folded in after the eight-minute knead; brush the top with water and press more seeds onto the surface before the final proof.
Where this loaf lives

The household tin loaf is the British baking baseline. Beeton's 1861 chapter on bread runs the same shape: strong flour, water, salt, yeast, a tin. The change since is in pace, not method — modern domestic ovens are hotter, modern yeasts work harder, and the fridge gives us a long flavour build her kitchen had to compensate for with sponges and pre-ferments. The recipe here is the household loaf with one modern adaptation: an overnight cold retard for the flavour she had to coax in other ways.
